title: Pride, Humility, and Serving Christ
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Pride, Humility, and Serving Christ - Pastor Tito Lyro - Luke 9_46-56.txt

outline.point: I. Review: Pride Is a Serious Sin
subpoints: A. Pride is an excessive preoccupation with self, rights, and importance
B. God hates pride and promises to judge the proud
C. Pride always ends in destruction (Proverbs 16:18)

outline.point: II. Pride Begins in the Heart (Luke 9:46-48)
subpoints: A. Jesus perceived the disciples' proud thoughts directly
B. Pride was the root of Eve's first sin in the garden
C. We must deal with pride at the heart level through confession

outline.point: III. Pride Is Fed by Competition; Humility by Cooperation
subpoints: A. The disciples competed to be greatest — Jesus silenced comparison with a child
B. All gifts and abilities come from Christ; we are stewards, not owners
C. Philippians 2:3-4: esteem others better than yourselves

outline.point: IV. Humble Love Is Tolerant with Fellow Servants (Luke 9:49-50)
subpoints: A. John forbade an exorcist not in their group — Jesus rebuked this
B. Religious zeal can mask pride — fighting for purity must be done in humble love
C. We often cloak pride under the banner of doctrinal correctness

outline.point: V. Humble Love Lets God Judge Those Who Oppose Us (Luke 9:51-56)
subpoints: A. James and John wanted to call down fire on the Samaritans — Jesus rebuked them
B. Humble love does not seek revenge; vengeance belongs to the Lord (Romans 12:19)
C. Living in humility and love is a powerful witness in a pride-saturated culture

summary:
This second sermon in the Pride and Humility series turns to the text of Luke 9:46-56 to show what effective service for Christ looks like when pride is mortified and humility takes root. After briefly reviewing the prior week's 32 manifestations of pride and the Bible's clear condemnation of it, Pastor Lyro examines the disciples' absurd argument about greatness — held in the very context of Jesus heading to the cross — to demonstrate that pride begins in the heart and must be confronted there. He highlights how pride is fed by competition and comparison, while humility thrives in cooperation, noting that since everything we have comes from Christ, there is no basis for boasting over others.

The sermon then moves through two episodes in the passage to show how humble love must characterize our treatment of both fellow servants and opponents of Christ. Regarding John's exclusion of the outside exorcist, Pastor Lyro warns that religious zeal often conceals pride, and that fighting for the purity of the church must always be conducted in humble love. Regarding James and John's desire to call fire down on the Samaritan village, the sermon teaches that humble love yields judgment to God, loving enemies rather than seeking revenge. The call to kill pride and grow in humility before 2022 is not merely ethical; it is deeply evangelical, rooted in living in the shadow of the blazing cross where our pride was crucified with Christ.
transcript:
## Introduction: Review and Vision

The beginning of the year provides the opportunity to venture into new projects with new goals. We like making resolutions. What I want us to do in this series — which was going to be three sermons but has now become four — is make 2022 the year in which we kill pride and grow in humility. I see that as a great need in my own heart, and it may be a blessing to you as well, because all of us struggle with pride at one point or another. I am convinced from the Scriptures that every relational issue we have — vertically, in our relationship with God, and horizontally, in our relationships with each other — is rooted in pride.

The 17th-century pastor Richard Hooker says: "Pride is a vice which cleaves so fast into the hearts of men, that if we were to strip ourselves of all faults one by one, we should undoubtedly find it the very last and hardest to put off." The truth is, brothers and sisters, that if we want to be effective servants of the Lord Jesus Christ, we must learn to put aside pride and be humble and loving — even toward those who oppose us. Pride and love are on opposite ends of the spectrum. You cannot be sinfully proud and loving at the same time. Love goes with humility, not with pride.

Let me take just a couple of minutes to review what we saw last week. Pride is an excessive preoccupation with self — with one's own importance, achievements, status, possessions, rights, and desire for personal justice and vengeance. The Bible uses several words to refer to it: insolence, presumptuousness, arrogance, conceit, high-mindedness, haughtiness. And God calls pride sin:

> *"A haughty look, a proud heart, and the plowing of the wicked are sin."* (Proverbs 21:4)

The Bible goes even further: God hates pride and the proud, because sin and the sinner always go together (Proverbs 6:16-19). And God promises to judge the proud. Proverbs 15:25 says, "The Lord will destroy the house of the proud." Notice it is not just the individual — the judgment touches those around the proud person as well.

As counterintuitive as it may seem, pride does not only manifest in self-worship and self-exaltation. It also manifests in self-pity, self-loathing, low self-esteem, and body image issues — all of these are rooted in pride. Last week I covered 32 different manifestations of pride as a diagnostic tool. We will not go through them again, but they are posted at olympiabp.net. The key thing to keep in mind: pride always ends in destruction. Proverbs 16:18:

> *"Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall."* (Proverbs 16:18)

If you think you can continue in pride and prosper, that is not going to happen. It brings destruction upon you and upon the relationships around you.

## I. Pride Begins in the Heart (Luke 9:46-48)

Now, turning to our passage, I want us to see that to be an effective servant of Christ, we must learn the lesson of humility. Look at verses 46-48:

> *"Then a dispute arose among them as to which of them would be greatest. And Jesus, perceiving the thought of their heart, took a little child and set him by Him, and said to them, 'Whoever receives this little child in My name receives Me; and whoever receives Me receives Him who sent Me. For he who is least among you all will be great.'"* (Luke 9:46-48)

When you pay attention to the context, the absurdity of this debate is heightened. The disciples are arguing about who is the greatest disciple — and this is happening in the context of the cross. In verse 44, Jesus had just announced that he was going to die. In verse 51, Luke says he set his face toward Jerusalem to meet his fate on the cross. And yet in this context, the disciples are trying to figure out who is going to take Christ's place, who will be the greatest of the twelve.

When we read a story like this, our temptation is to say, "Those disciples — I can't believe they would do that. We are Christians; we don't do that." But before we go there, we need to acknowledge that we struggle against the same things the disciples did. We lose track of the cross of Christ and turn our gaze inwardly. At that point, we start thinking we are something great, that we should be the greatest in the kingdom of God.

### Pride Must Be Dealt with at the Heart Level

In verse 47, Luke tells us that Jesus *perceived the thought of their heart*. There may have been obvious outward signs of their bickering — but at the bottom of it, Jesus knew what was in their hearts. And what was there was pride.

Pride was the root of the very first sin. Eve thought she could be like God if she disobeyed him and ate the forbidden fruit. Eve's sin began long before she took a bite of that apple. It began in her heart. And pride is at the root of almost all sin — because we think we know better than God who has given us his commandments. We wrongly think we know what is best for us, even when it goes against what God has clearly said.

Try this exercise: if you have ever thought, said, or acted according to the statement, "I know the Bible says __, but..." — you have acted according to the belief that you know better than God. And if you have ever sinned, you have done exactly that. So to deal with pride, we must confess our selfish rebellion against God and humble ourselves before him.

We tend to be like Linus from the Peanuts cartoon strip. In one strip, Lucy asks Linus what he is going to be when he grows up, and he says he wants to be a humble country doctor. He will live in the city, hop in his Ferrari every day, drive to the country, and heal everyone. In the last frame, he says he will be a world-famous humble little country doctor. So often in our hearts, we want to be a world-famous humble servant of Christ. But that is not what we are called to do. Remember the testimony of John the Baptist? "He must increase, I must decrease." That is what we are called to do.

## II. Pride Is Fed by Competition; Humility by Cooperation

We also see in this passage that pride is fed by competition, but humility is fed by cooperation.

If you do not know it already, I am one of the most — actually, I am the most competitive person I am aware of. I love winning. Among the disciples, there can only be one greatest disciple, so each one competes for that position by comparing himself to the others. But Jesus thoroughly disarms their way of thinking. He makes no comparison among the twelve or between them and anyone else. He brings a little child.

What does that say to them? What would you have to offer a little child? Nothing. That child has absolutely nothing to offer Jesus — and yet he received that child. Similarly, Paul says to the divisive Corinthians: "What do you have that you did not receive?" The point of bringing the child is this: every one of you was like this little child when Jesus received you. You had absolutely nothing to offer. So what is it that you have now, that you claim makes you the greatest, that you did not receive from him? God has given us everything we are and everything we have. We are only stewards of it for his sake.

And here is a side benefit of that way of thinking: it is really easy to give away other people's money. It is easy to use other people's stuff to bless people. And that is exactly our situation — everything we have belongs to Jesus. Our houses, our assets, our gifts — we are just managers and stewards. And since it all comes from Jesus Christ himself, it is ridiculous for us to be puffed up over our own gifts or abilities or to look down on others who do not have what we have.

There is a lot of jealousy in the church of Jesus Christ. When somebody succeeds in ministry, when a big-name pastor has a big church, it is easy to be jealous about what the Lord is doing through them instead of being thankful for it. But rather than being jealous because someone has a spiritual experience or recognition we lack, we should rejoice — because the Lord's team has won. As Paul says in Philippians 2:3-4:

> *"Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself. Let each of you look out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others."* (Philippians 2:3-4)

## III. Pride Is Fed by Association with the Important; Humility by the Lowly

We also see in this passage that pride is fed by our association with the important, but humility is fed by association with the lowly.

This episode in Luke 9 is taking place in the second year of Jesus' public ministry — the year of his greatest popularity. Crowds are following him everywhere. The disciples are excited about being with Christ because he is famous and important. And yet, a year later, when he goes to the cross, the disciples run away. They do not want to be associated with the lowly anymore.

Brothers and sisters, the only thing that makes us anything is our association with Jesus — not because of anything good in us, but only by his undeserved grace. If you are something, you are something because you are associated with Christ. He did not choose you because he saw something great in you. Read 1 Corinthians 1 — it will really help your self-esteem. God did not choose the able, the smart, the wealthy, or the influencer. He chose those who could not do without him. And that is us today.

Humility grows when we focus on Christ's presence and on the cross. Christ knows every proud and jealous thought we entertain — every last one of them. If we kept that in mind, we would be quick to judge our proud thoughts the second we recognize them, because the second they enter our hearts, Jesus is aware of them.

### Living in the Shadow of the Cross

This debate among the disciples is happening in the context of the cross — verses 44 and 51 both tell us that. And if we would keep in view the suffering that Jesus went through to save us from our sins, how could we go on exalting ourselves over others?

"Do you know how great I am?" So great that it took God the Son in the flesh, on the cross, to redeem me. That means I am not great at all. We are so sinful, so corrupted, that it took God in the flesh on the cross to redeem us. It was my pride, my selfishness, that put the sinless Savior on the cross. As Isaac Watts wrote:

> *"When I survey the wondrous cross on which the Prince of glory died, my richest gain I count but loss, and pour contempt on all my pride."*

To kill pride, we must live — as John Stott said — in the shadow of the blazing cross, catching the sparks that come from it, knowing that our pride was crucified with Christ.

## IV. Humble Love for Both Friends and Opponents (Luke 9:49-56)

The passage continues in verses 49-56, and we see that a key element of humility is love for others. There are two groups represented in these verses: fellow servants of Christ in verses 49-50, and those who are opposed to Christ in verses 51-56. We need to show love to both groups. The form of love may look different, but it must be present — and you can only do that if you die to pride and live in humility.

### Humble Love Is Tolerant of Fellow Servants

In verses 49-50, John says, "Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we forbade him because he does not follow with us." Jesus replies:

> *"Do not forbid him, for he who is not against us is on our side."* (Luke 9:50)

I hesitate to use the word "tolerant" here because it is so misunderstood today. In our culture, tolerance has come to mean acceptance and praise of everything. But tolerance in its actual meaning simply means: to disagree, and yet not destroy the person — to disagree and yet be kind. That is the correct sense, and that is how humble love must operate toward fellow servants of Jesus Christ.

The warning of this verse is that we often cloak our pride in religious zeal. We put down other brothers and sisters because they differ with us, and we hide behind the claim that we are fighting for the purity of the church — when really we are just being proud. We do need to contend for the purity of the church. But contending for purity is done in humble love.

### Humble Love Lets God Deal with Opponents

In verses 51-56, Jesus sends messengers ahead into a Samaritan village, and the village refuses to receive him. James and John say, "Lord, do you want us to command fire to come down from heaven and consume them, just as Elijah did?" And Jesus rebukes them.

I am so grateful for the disciples — it warms my heart that they did the same foolish things I do. Jesus says, "What are you doing? Nobody is going to die today. I will deal with it. God will deal with it. You just go and proclaim the gospel."

James and John thought they were acting in the spirit of Elijah, who called down fire from heaven on the messengers of King Ahaziah (2 Kings 1:9-16). They could cite chapter and verse for why those people deserved judgment. But they were wrong. Jesus came to seek and to save the lost, not to destroy them.

Rejection is always difficult to handle. But if our Lord was rejected, we can expect the same treatment from a wicked world. In fact, if you have never been rejected by people because of your faith in Christ, it is worth examining where you are. Our Lord said that one thing you can surely expect in this life is persecution.

Humble love lets God be the judge of those who oppose us. As Paul writes:

> *"Beloved, do not avenge yourselves, but rather give place to wrath; for it is written, 'Vengeance is Mine, I will repay,' says the Lord. Therefore if your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him a drink; for in so doing you will heap coals of fire on his head."* (Romans 12:19-20)

Think of someone with whom you have a conflict — that is your enemy, in the biblical description of the term. And how does humble love call you to act toward that enemy? Love them. Let God avenge you. If all you care about in a conflict is to get revenge, to have your name vindicated, to prove you are right — then you are acting in pride, not humility.

And notice: in that very last sentence of verse 56, "they went to another village." Can you imagine? That village missed seeing the Lord of glory with their own eyes, even in the proclamation of the gospel from the Lord of glory himself. That was a great judgment that came upon them.

## Conclusion

Brothers and sisters, we live in a culture seething with anger that has elevated pride to a virtue. We can have great opportunities for witness if we learn the lessons of humility and love. If you are mistreated and you respond in humility and in love, you will stand out as a light in the darkness of our culture.

2022 is before us. May it be the year in which we die to pride — the pride of life — and humbly live in submission to God and in unity with one another, displayed in humble love." We're going to read Luke chapter 9, St. Louis 46. We're taking a break from our first John series to talk about a vision for 2022 that includes dying to pride and leaving in humility. And Jesus perceiving the thought of their heart took a little child and set him by him and set to them, whoever receives this little child, the minding, receives me. And whoever receives me receives him who sent me. For he who is least among you all will be great. Now John answered and said, Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name and we forbade him because he does not follow with us. But Jesus said to him, to not forbid him, for he who is not against us is on our side. Now it came to pass when the time had come for him to be received up that he steadfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem. And set messengers before his face. And as they went, they entered a village of the Samaritans to prepare for him. By they did not receive him because his face was set for the journey to Jerusalem. But he turned and rebuked them and said, you do not know what manner of spirit you are of, for the Son of Man did not come to destroy man's lives, but to save them and it went to another village. This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, I have not thank you for your word. We pray that as we consider it this morning, you need to work mightly in our hearts to love you and to follow you for asking Jesus name, amen. Please be seated.
